context.description: A philosophy book chapter section examines dreams as a way to question conventional ideas of consciousness. The author rejects vague esoteric claims and argues that dream experience reveals a discontinuity between waking awareness and what remains outside it.
normalization_notes: Translated German headings into English-controlled semantic summary only; original title retained in source-derived work title.
Classified as chapter_section because the page contains chapter and section headings plus continuous argumentative prose.

rows.paragraph_text: The author positions the discussion against vague modern esoteric claims and says experience must be personal rather than blindly believed. The section introduces dreams as a way to examine what lies beyond the horizon of waking awareness.

Dreams are described as fantastic worlds that ignore physical law, causality, and continuity, making them appear grotesque from the standpoint of waking consciousness. The text uses this contrast to argue that dream life exposes how much waking awareness forgets about what falls outside it.

content.free_text: book_page book_body_content chapter_section Chapter 4: When Consciousness Makes Jumps. Section on dreams and other delusions. Philosophical discussion of consciousness, dream experience, and the limits of waking awareness.
